summary: DeVault recounts browsing the interactive flight map on a British Airways flight to Japan, treating the map's depiction of international border disputes as the airline's official political positions. He notes the absence of open source license disclosures despite likely use of open source software, then systematically catalogs how the map handles disputed territories including Palestine/Israel, Ukraine/Russia, Taiwan, Kashmir, Sudan, and others. The post uses dry humor to highlight how seemingly neutral technical decisions about map rendering inevitably encode political stances. Palestine is absent as a labeled country while Israel appears, Taiwan lacks a country label, and Ukraine is shown with undisputed borders including Crimea.
key_points: The in-flight entertainment system showed no open source license disclosures despite likely containing open source software, with only an OpenStreetMap copyright visible

The map's treatment of disputed territories varies inconsistently: Ukraine shown whole including Crimea, Palestine unlabeled while Israel appears, Taiwan's cities labeled but the island itself unnamed
British Airways' map implicitly endorses the Oslo Accords by showing West Bank areas but not recognizing Palestine as a country
Technical decisions about map rendering are inherently political, as every border drawn or label omitted reflects a position on sovereignty disputes
money_quotes: I am going to assume that all of the borders shown in this map are reflective of the official opinion of British Airways on various international disputes.
I went directly to Palestine. Or rather, I went to Israel, because Palestine doesn't exist on this map.

Given that this is British Airways, part of me was surprised not to see the whole area simply labelled Mandatory Palestine, but it is interesting to know that British Airways officially supports the Oslo Accords.

I entertained myself on the long flight by browsing the interactive flight map on the back of my neighbor's seat and trying to figure out how the poor developer who implemented this map solved the thorny problems that displaying a world map implies.
I believe the industry term is 'infotainment system', but if you ever catch me saying that with a straight face then I have been replaced with an imposter and you should contact the authorities.
key_insight: Every world map is a political document, and the technical choices made by an airline's flight map developers inevitably encode positions on sovereignty disputes whether they intend to or not.
